title: CSR: Terminology & Abbreviations You'll Actually Use
description: The medical words and abbreviations a CSR hears and records every day — focused on accurate spelling, scheduling, and communication, not clinical interpretation.

blocks.content:
You don't diagnose, but you transcribe and schedule around medical terms constantly. Recognizing and spelling them correctly keeps records accurate.

Common terms at the front desk:
• Spay (OHE) and neuter — the routine sterilization surgeries.
• Vaccine / booster — preventive shots, often on a schedule.
• Wellness exam — routine check-up.
• Recheck — a follow-up visit.
• Dental / dental cleaning — oral care, often under anesthesia.
• Anesthesia — being put under for a procedure.
• Bloodwork / labs — diagnostic tests.

You'll also hear body-system words. You don't need to interpret them — just spell and record them correctly. When unsure of a spelling, ask; a misspelled medication in a record is a real problem.
When you hear an unfamiliar medical word, ask for the spelling rather than guessing. 'Could you spell that medication for me?' is professional, not embarrassing.

blocks.front: What does a CSR need to do with medical terms — interpret them or record them accurately?
blocks.back: Record them accurately (correct spelling, right patient) — interpretation is the medical team's job.

blocks.question: A doctor's note mentions a medication you've never heard of. Before entering it, you should:

options.feedback: A misspelled medication can cause real errors; never guess.
Correct — accuracy on medications matters for safety; verify it.
Omitting it creates an incomplete record; confirm and enter it.

lessons.title: Shorthand You'll See on Schedules and Records

blocks.content:
Abbreviations fill veterinary schedules and records. Here are ones a CSR commonly encounters:

Scheduling/visit types: OHE (spay), recheck, wellness, vax (vaccines), dental.

General record shorthand you'll see: Hx (history), Dx (diagnosis), Tx (treatment), Rx (prescription), Px (prognosis), PE (physical exam), wt (weight), TPR (temperature/pulse/respiration), WNL (within normal limits).

Appointment notes: HBC (hit by car — an emergency), ADR ('ain't doing right' — a vague illness note).

You don't need every clinical abbreviation, but recognizing these helps you read schedules, route appointments, and understand notes without constantly asking.
Recognize HBC (Hit By Car) instantly — it signals an emergency or urgent case, and how you handle that appointment matters.

blocks.front: What does 'HBC' mean and why should a CSR recognize it?
blocks.back: Hit By Car — it signals an emergency/urgent case that needs appropriate prioritization.

blocks.front: What does 'OHE' refer to on a schedule?

blocks.question: You see 'ADR' in an appointment note. What does it tell you?

options.feedback: ADR isn't a diagnosis — it means 'ain't doing right,' a vague sign of illness.

options.text: The pet is generally unwell in a nonspecific way ('ain't doing right')
options.feedback: Correct — ADR flags a vague 'something's off' presentation.
ADR has nothing to do with cancellation.
